title: SEGMENT 11 — Drosophila for behavior and neuroscience: circadian rhythms and the 2017 Nobel

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Drosophila for behavior and neuroscience: circadian rhythms and the 2017 Nobel

If the fly showed us the genetics of body plan, it also showed us, over the following decades, the genetics of behavior — the most surprising biology to come out of an animal that, intuitively, you wouldn't expect to do anything interesting.
Flies don't have brains, you might think.
Flies are barely conscious.
What could a fly possibly teach us about behavior, learning, and memory?
The answer, as it turned out, is: a lot.
And the cleanest example, and the one that earned a Nobel Prize in 2017, is the genetics of circadian rhythm.
Every animal on Earth has a roughly 24-hour internal clock.
You feel sleepy at night and awake during the day, even in the absence of external cues, because your body has an internal oscillator that runs on a 24-hour period.
The question that occupied a small group of scientists starting in the 1970s was: what genes encode this clock?
Where does the 24-hour period actually come from, at the molecular level?
In the early 1970s, Ronald Konopka and Seymour Benzer ran a screen in flies.
They mutagenized flies, they raised the offspring in constant darkness, and they looked for flies whose eclosion — the time at which adult flies emerge from their pupal cases — was off-schedule.
Wild-type flies eclose preferentially in the early morning.
Konopka and Benzer found mutants that eclosed at random times, mutants that eclosed too fast, mutants that eclosed too slowly.
They mapped the mutations and discovered that they were all in one gene, which they named **period, or per**.
For the next twenty years, period was the leading edge of circadian biology.
In the 1980s, Jeffrey Hall and Michael Rosbash at Brandeis cloned the period gene and showed that the period protein levels in fly brains oscillated with a 24-hour rhythm.

Make protein, levels go up, somehow this represses period transcription, transcript goes down, protein goes down, repression released, transcript goes up — a self-sustaining negative feedback loop with a 24-hour period.
Then came more clock genes from more screens.
timeless** — Michael Young's lab — encoded a partner of period.
doubletime** — also Young — encoded a kinase that phosphorylated period, regulating its turnover rate.
clock and cycle** — discovered later — encoded the transcription factors that drove period and timeless expression.

Together, these genes formed a coherent molecular oscillator: a transcription-translation negative feedback loop in which CLOCK and CYCLE turn on period and timeless, the resulting proteins accumulate, dimerize, and repress their own transcription, then get degraded by DOUBLETIME, and the cycle restarts.
The 24-hour period emerges from the kinetics of this loop — how fast the proteins accumulate, how fast they get phosphorylated, how fast they get degraded.
It's a molecular oscillator, encoded by half a dozen genes, that runs continuously in every cell of the fly.
And — you can probably guess where this is going — the same loop runs in your cells right now.
Mammals have homologs of period, timeless, clock, and cycle, doing essentially the same job, with essentially the same architecture.
Your suprachiasmatic nucleus — the small region of your hypothalamus that orchestrates daily rhythms — runs on Per/Cry/Clock/Bmal1, the mammalian versions of the fly clock genes.
Jet lag, shift work, seasonal affective disorder, and the metabolic consequences of disrupted sleep are all downstream of this molecular oscillator.
The analogy: **fly chronobiology is the place where the clock was opened.
period, timeless, doubletime — the clock genes were all found in flies first, then conserved across mammals.** Hall, Rosbash, and Young shared the Nobel Prize in 2017 for this work.
The fly was, again, the organism in which a universal mechanism was first dissected at the molecular level.
Once dissected, it generalized.

Beyond circadian rhythm, flies have been used to dissect learning and memory (the gene dunce, a cAMP phosphodiesterase, was the first learning gene found in any animal — by Seymour Benzer's lab in the 1970s), courtship behavior (the gene fruitless specifies male-typical courtship), aggression, sleep, addiction.
Every category of complex behavior that we used to think required a vertebrate brain turns out to have a fly version that can be genetically dissected.
The big idea: fly genetics turned behavior from a black-box phenomenon into a molecular biology question.
The clock genes are the cleanest example, but the same logic runs through learning, sleep, and many other behaviors.
The fly proved that even complex behavior has molecular handles you can grab.
Now let's leave invertebrates entirely and meet the vertebrate that became the workhorse of developmental biology — the zebrafish.
